preFix.title: Spinning a bulky-weight singles on the wheel
preFix.subtitle: Low twist-per-inch, a short drafting zone, and a large bobbin for a true Super Chunky singles
preFix.excerpt: Spinning a Super Chunky (bulky) singles on a wheel requires settings that are the opposite of fine-yarn work: a very low twist ratio, a very short drafting zone, and a large-capacity bobbin. The key judgement call is knowing when the twist is set in the thick yarn without it kinked. A simple hang test gives the answer: the yarn should hold its shape when tension is released but not snap into a hard corkscrew.
preFix.sourceNotes: Wheel settings for bulky singles (low ratio, large bobbin, low TPI) are documented across modern spinning texts and wheel manufacturer guidance for jumbo flyer setups. The hang test for twist assessment is standard practice taught in spinning workshops.

attrs.body: Keep loose hair and clothing back from the drive band and flyer. The flyer turns fast; tie hair back and roll sleeves.
attrs.title: Wheel safety

content.text: Spinning bulky yarn on a wheel requires a different mental model from everyday fine-yarn spinning. Every setting that goes up for fine work goes down for bulky: lower twist ratio, lower
twist per inch
, shorter drafting zone, and slower treadle relative to drafting speed. The bobbin fills very quickly with a thick
singles
and a standard bobbin may only hold 50-80 g of bulky yarn. Most wheels offer a jumbo flyer and bobbin kit: check your wheel manufacturer's options.
What you need

items.name: Prepared roving or batt, any breed with good crimp (Corriedale, BFL, or similar)
Spinning wheel with jumbo flyer and bobbin (or low-ratio whorl, minimum bobbin capacity 150 g)
Soft tape measure for WPI checks

content.text: Setting the wheel

Step 1: Fit the lowest-ratio whorl available on your wheel. A ratio of 4:1 or 5:1 is about right for a Super Chunky; some jumbo flyer kits go as low as 3:1. This means the flyer makes only 3-5 full rotations for each treadle stroke, producing very few twists per inch even at a moderate treadle speed.

Step 2: Set a moderate take-up. Bulky yarn is heavier than fine yarn and needs more pull to seat onto the bobbin. Start with the brake band slightly firmer than you would for DK weight and adjust after the first metre of spinning.
Drafting

Step 3: Work with a short drafting zone: approximately 6-8 cm between hands. The thick strand needs only a short draft to reach the planned diameter; a long-draw approach with bulky fibre tends to thin the strand too much in places.
Step 4: Draft wide strips of roving: 15-20 mm or wider, depending on the planned finished diameter. Check the planned
WPI

before spinning and pre-draft a test strip to the approximate thickness. A Super Chunky singles wraps at 4-6 WPI on the finished skein; the fresh-off-bobbin strand will be slightly thicker before wet-finishing.

Step 5: Keep treadle speed low and drafting speed moderate. The ratio is already very low; treadling fast adds unnecessary twist. Treadle at a relaxed walking pace and draft at a rate that keeps up with the take-up without rushing.
The twist-set hang test

Step 6: Every few minutes, stop treadling and let 30-40 cm of yarn hang free from the orifice. The correct result: the yarn hangs straight or with only a gentle curl. If it hangs straight and limp, the twist is possibly too low. If it kinks into a corkscrew, the twist is too high. Adjust by treadling slightly faster or slower relative to your drafting pace.

A bulky singles for single-ply use needs slightly more twist than one that will be plied. Plying reduces the overall twist angle. Aim for a singles that gently twists back on itself rather than hanging completely straight. Some twist is also lost during wet-finishing, so a little extra at the spinning stage is worthwhile.
Finishing

Wind off onto a niddy-noddy or a large warping frame: a standard niddy-noddy wraps a small skein quickly with bulky yarn. Tie in four places. Soak in warm water for 20 minutes, press in a towel, and hang under moderate weight tension to set the twist. A Super Chunky singles benefits from a heavier weight during drying (500 g-1 kg) to reduce the natural spiral. Measure WPI after drying.
Common problems

items.fix: Spin in batches to match the bobbin capacity, or fit a jumbo bobbin. If neither is available, wind each partial bobbin onto a ball and join the next section: the join is easy to hide in thick yarn.
items.cause: Standard bobbins hold far less bulky yarn by weight than they do fine yarn. A 150 g bobbin may only hold 80-100 g of a thick singles.
items.problem: The bobbin fills before all the fibre is spun

items.fix: Reduce the treadle rate or increase the draft speed for the next batch. Soak the current skein and hang under heavy weight tension for 24 hours: this relaxes most of the excess twist.
items.cause: Too much twist per inch for the yarn diameter. The thick strand has too much active twist and springs back on itself.
items.problem: The finished skein kinked hard when taken off the niddy-noddy

items.fix: Pre-draft the entire roving supply to a consistent width before sitting at the wheel. Check the width of each strip against the measuring tape: aim for a consistent 15-20 mm.
items.cause: The roving strip width was inconsistent, or the drafting zone length varied.
items.problem: The yarn diameter varies a lot throughout
